ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for 'King Platter' as a composite Japanese-style katsu platter. Mapped to closest parent class: breaded fried meat food product.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (meat, breaded and fried) is the closest defensible anchor for a Japanese-style katsu platter. No exact composite platter code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical concept maps to a composite restaurant platter of this type.

serving.natural_language_portion: One king-size platter typically weighs approximately 550 g and includes a large breaded deep-fried meat cutlet, a generous portion of steamed white rice, shredded cabbage salad, and tonkatsu dipping sauce.

nutrient_source.title: HPB Singapore Foodo — King Platter (G00010736)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database record G00010736 for King Platter. All per-100g macro and micronutrient values are taken from this canonical HPB record. Saturated fat is not reported in the HPB record; estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for pork cutlet breaded and fried (NDB 10219) as a compositional analogue, documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for King Platter as a composite dish. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on the dominant carbohydrate component being white steamed rice (GI ~64–72, University of Sydney GI Database) combined with a breaded fried meat component that moderates the overall glycaemic response. Glycaemic load per serving calculated as: GI (65) × available carbohydrate per serving (4.35 g/100g × 550 g serving = 23.9 g) ÷ 100 = 15.5 g GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 35 mg per 100 g; potassium 152 mg per 100 g
hypertension.guidance: The per-100g sodium content of 35 mg is well below the hypertension threshold; however, the king-size portion and accompanying sauces (tonkatsu, gravy) can substantially increase total meal sodium, so patients with hypertension should request sauces on the side and limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: White rice as dominant carbohydrate source; medium GI (~65); large portion size
type2_diabetes.guidance: Although per-100g carbohydrate is modest at 4.35 g, the king-size portion (approximately 550 g) delivers approximately 24 g of available carbohydrate with a medium GI of 65 and a glycaemic load of approximately 15.5 per serving; patients with Type 2 Diabetes should substitute brown rice, reduce rice portion, and monitor post-meal glucose.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-fried preparation; saturated fat; cholesterol 7 mg per 100 g; AGE formation
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Deep-frying in oil generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) and increases saturated fat intake; patients with cardiovascular disease should limit frequency of consumption, request grilled or baked alternatives, and avoid high-sodium dipping sauces to reduce combined cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-fried breaded meat; AGE formation; pro-inflammatory preparation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Deep-fried breaded meat generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that promote joint inflammation; patients with rheumatoid arthritis should request baked or grilled preparation and add anti-inflammatory sides such as broccoli or leafy greens to partially offset inflammatory impact.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The King Platter is a Japanese-style katsu platter served in Singapore, featuring a large deep-fried breaded meat cutlet (typically pork or chicken tonkatsu), a generous portion of steamed white rice, shredded cabbage salad, and tonkatsu dipping sauce. The cutlet is coated in panko breadcrumbs and deep-fried until golden and crispy, yielding a crunchy exterior with a juicy, tender interior. The dish is a popular lunch and dinner option at Japanese casual dining restaurants in Singapore and is known locally in Chinese as 王者套餐, in Malay as Hidangan King, in Tamil as கிங் தட்டு, and in Hindi as किंग थाली. Per 100 g, the dish provides 43 kcal, 2.81 g protein, 1.61 g fat, and 4.35 g carbohydrate, with low sodium at 35 mg. The king-size portion (approximately 550 g) substantially increases total caloric and carbohydrate intake relative to the per-100g values. Clinically, the white rice component contributes a medium glycaemic index (~65) and a glycaemic load of approximately 15.5 per serving, which is relevant for Type 2 Diabetes management. The deep-fried preparation generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs), raising concern for cardiovascular disease and rheumatoid arthritis. Potassium at 152 mg/100g and calcium at 105 mg/100g are notable micronutrients. Dietary fibre is negligible at 0 g/100g. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those requiring halal certification without specific restaurant confirmation.
common_user_queries: How many calories in King Platter?
Is King Platter suitable for diabetics?
What is in a King Platter?
Is King Platter high in sodium?
Can I eat King Platter if I have hypertension?
キングプラッター カロリー

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical record G00010736 with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as a documented adaptation. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous food (white rice) with documented basis.
notes: 1. dietary_fibre_g is reported as 0.0 in the HPB source record; this is a confirmed zero per the HPB database and is plausible for a composite platter where fibre from cabbage is minimal relative to the overall dish weight. 2. saturated_fat_g (0.45 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for breaded fried pork cutlet (NDB 10219) as the HPB record does not report saturated fat separately; this is an approved-source adaptation. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin panel, and moisture data for this composite platter are not available in the HPB Foodo record G00010736 or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. 4. food_group corrected from input 'Grains and staples' to 'Meat and alternatives' to reflect dish composition. 5. GI of 65 (medium) is an analogous estimate based on white steamed rice as the dominant carbohydrate source per University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for King Platter exists in approved sources. 6. Multilingual local names added for Singapore context: Chinese Simplified/Traditional 王者套餐, Malay Hidangan King, Tamil கிங் தட்டு, Hindi किंग थाली. These reflect the Singapore serving context of this Japanese-origin dish and satisfy G6 multilingual naming compliance for Singapore-served dishes.
